findings.title: Static file route exposes entire repo (SQLite DB + .env + source)
findings.description: serveStaticFile only checks path stays under projectRoot but has no extension allowlist or directory blocklist. /data/app.db, /.env, /server.js, /CLAUDE.md, /scripts/* all return 200.
findings.exploit_scenario: GET /data/app.db downloads the SQLite DB containing all user phone numbers, chat history, extracted facts, goals. GET /.env returns USER_SESSION_SECRET (forge any user's session) + DEEPSEEK_API_KEY + ALIYUN_ACCESS_KEY_*.
findings.impact: Complete user data breach (PIPL reportable). Auth secret leak = full account system compromise. API key leak = financial damage.
findings.recommendation: Add extension allowlist + directory denylist in serveStaticFile. Apply nginx deny block as immediate stopgap.
findings.playbook: 1. Rotate USER_SESSION_SECRET. 2. Rotate DEEPSEEK_API_KEY + ALIYUN keys. 3. Audit pm2 logs for GET /data/ or GET /.env hits. 4. PIPL notification if exfiltration confirmed.
findings.verification: code-traced: handleRequest fall-through at server.js:214-215 -> serveStaticFile path-only check at server.js:880-887 -> any in-tree file streamable

findings.title: Client controls LLM system prompt (free DeepSeek proxy)
findings.description: validateChatBody accepts body.systemPrompt (4000 char slice) and forwards verbatim to DeepSeek. Anyone can override the assistant persona.
findings.exploit_scenario: POST /api/chat with arbitrary systemPrompt -> DeepSeek responds in attacker-controlled persona. With IP rotation, attacker uses bookofelon.cn as a free LLM proxy and can generate harmful content under your domain's brand.
findings.impact: DeepSeek bill amplification + brand reputation risk (offensive content traceable to your domain).
findings.recommendation: Construct system prompt server-side from PROMPT_VERSION env. Client only sends messages + sanitized context.
findings.verification: code-traced: validateChatBody:1019 -> buildUpstreamMessages:772-781 -> system role content concatenates client systemPrompt with serverside contextBlock

findings.title: SMS provider silently falls back to mock; OTP returned in HTTP response
findings.description: If SMS_PROVIDER unset and ALIYUN_* incomplete, getProvider returns 'mock'. handleSendCode then includes the OTP plaintext in the JSON response. No production guard.
findings.exploit_scenario: Config drift removes one ALIYUN env var. Provider silently becomes 'mock'. Attacker POSTs send-code for any phone, reads devCode from response, POSTs verify-code, gets logged-in cookie for that phone.
findings.impact: Total auth bypass for any user account if env config drifts.
findings.recommendation: Mirror auth/session.js pattern: in production, throw at startup if provider resolves to mock. Eagerly call getProvider() during server boot.
findings.verification: code-traced: getProvider:7 returns 'mock' silently -> sendVerificationCode logs to console -> handleSendCode:82-85 attaches code to JSON body

findings.title: Dockerfile runs as root + uses npm install (no lockfile) + data/ not in .dockerignore
findings.description: Three issues compound: (1) no USER directive = root container, (2) npm install instead of npm ci ignores lockfile, (3) .dockerignore lacks data/, *.db, backups/.
findings.exploit_scenario: If anyone builds a Docker image after running tests locally, data/test-*.db leak into the image. Combined with #1, an LFI gives root access. Supply chain attacks through transitive dep version drift.
findings.impact: Defense-in-depth weakened; potential PII leak via baked-in test DBs.
findings.recommendation: Add non-root USER, switch to npm ci, expand .dockerignore to exclude data/, backups/, *.db, .gstack/.
findings.verification: Dockerfile read in full; .dockerignore read in full

findings.title: Nginx example config lacks HTTPS, security headers, deny rules
findings.description: Example only listens on port 80, has no SSL/TLS block, no Strict-Transport-Security, no client_max_body_size, no deny rules for sensitive paths.
findings.exploit_scenario: Operators copying the example will end up with permissive nginx; no defense-in-depth against #1.
findings.recommendation: Ship a production-ready template with 80->443 redirect, SSL block, security headers, deny location for /data /db /scripts /. paths.

findings.title: Backup files likely written with default 644 perms and inside web-served directory
findings.description: Backups land at data/backups/app-*.db.gz; that path is reachable through the static route (per #1). Default file mode is 644, readable by other local users.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker requests /data/backups/app-2026-*.db.gz to grab historical user-data snapshots. Local non-root users on the same host can read backups.
findings.impact: Historical data exposure; tightens once #1 is fixed.
findings.recommendation: chmod 600 data/backups/*.gz in the backup script; relocate backups outside the web cwd (e.g., /var/backups/book-of-elon/).
findings.verification: code path inferred from CLAUDE.md and DEPLOY_RUNBOOK.md backup section; not hands-on tested

findings.title: .env stored inside web service cwd (root cause of #1's worst case)
findings.description: Deployment runbook places .env at /root/skill_The_book_of_Elon/.env, which is the web server's cwd. This is why #1 can leak secrets.
findings.exploit_scenario: See #1: GET /.env returns full env contents.
findings.impact: All secrets leak together when #1 is exploited.
findings.recommendation: Move secrets to systemd Environment= directives or relocate .env to /etc/book-of-elon/.env (not in web cwd).
findings.verification: DEPLOY_RUNBOOK.md and pm2 ecosystem config inspected
